_note: Trip-relevant glossary. Each entry: term, optional aliases (alt spellings users might type), category, and a 1-2 sentence definition.

terms.def: Natural hot-spring bath. Either communal (sex-segregated) or in-room. You wash thoroughly at the seated showers first, then soak naked — no swimsuits, no soap in the bath.
An open-air outdoor onsen, usually with a view. The dawn or nighttime soak in mist is the classic ryokan ritual.
Traditional Japanese inn — tatami rooms, futon laid out at night, in-room or private-dining kaiseki dinner, onsen access. Typically 1 or 2 nights only; you're paying for the ritual, not the room.
Multi-course (8–12 courses) Japanese haute cuisine, seasonally composed. Each course is a deliberate counterpoint to the last; no substitutions; the kitchen plans the arc.
A kaiseki course of small seasonal bites on a single plate, set early in the meal to establish the season's theme.
Light cotton robe provided by the ryokan. Worn around the building, to dinner, and to the onsen. Left side over right (right-over-left is for the dead — don't reverse).
Woven rush-grass floor mats. Rooms are sized in tatami count (a 6-tatami room is ~10 m²). No shoes, no slippers — only socks or bare feet.
Thin folded mattress laid directly on tatami at bedtime. Folded and stowed each morning. Comfortable once you're used to a firm sleeping surface.
A private/reservable family bath at a ryokan — book a 45-min slot. Useful if you want to soak together or skip the communal etiquette.
The female head of a ryokan or traditional restaurant. The face of the establishment, manages the dining ritual.
Traditional wooden townhouse, mostly Kyoto. Narrow street frontage, deep interior, often a small courtyard garden. Many are now cafés, shops, or boutique hotels.
Steep-thatched-roof farmhouse style of the Hida region (Shirakawa-go, Hida-no-Sato). Beams tied with rope, no nails, attic for silkworms. The roof shape resembles hands in prayer (gasshō).
Thick-walled white-plaster storehouse, fireproof. Many Takayama sake breweries are kura interiors.
Public bathhouse (not a natural hot spring — that's onsen). SCAI The Bathhouse in Yanaka is a converted sento.
The gate marking the entry to a Shinto shrine. Walking through one means you're entering sacred ground; bow slightly. Don't walk down the exact center — that's the kami's path.
Shinto shrine. Suffix on many place names (Meiji Jingu = Meiji Shrine, Nezu Jinja = Nezu Shrine).
Suffix meaning Buddhist temple. Kiyomizu-dera, Saiho-ji, Tofuku-ji. Shrines (jinja) are Shinto; temples (dera/ji) are Buddhist — the gate, smell, and statues differ.
The main outer gate of a Buddhist temple. Often huge, oldest surviving wooden structures in Japan (Tofuku-ji's sanmon is from 1425).
The abbot's quarters in a Zen temple. Usually surrounded by rock or moss gardens viewed from a veranda. Tofuku-ji's Hojo gardens (Mirei Shigemori, 1939) are the modernist landmark.
Dry-landscape Zen garden — raked white gravel for water, large stones for islands or mountains. The famous "rock garden" type at Ryoan-ji, Tofuku-ji.
The bodhisattva of compassion, often depicted as a woman, hundreds of arms. Sanjusangen-do has 1,001 wooden Kannon statues in a row.
Small stone bodhisattva statue, protector of children and travelers. The little stone figures in moss at Sanzen-in are warabe-jizo (child jizo).
Faceless red baby doll from the Takayama / Hida region — a regional talisman, often given to children and brides. No face on purpose: the wearer projects their own emotion.
Souvenir food gifts — boxed wagashi, regional snacks (hato-sablé, Tokyo banana). Strong gifting culture; you bring some home from each region.
Deep-fried breaded pork cutlet — loin (rosu) or fillet (hire). Served with shredded cabbage, rice, miso soup. Maisen Aoyama Honten is the Tokyo classic.
Skewers of meat, vegetables, or seafood breaded and deep-fried. Hantei in Nezu serves it course-by-course in a 100-year-old wooden building.
Buckwheat noodles, served cold (zaru, seiro) with dipping sauce, or hot (kake) in broth. Honke Owariya (Kyoto) has been doing it since 1465.
Thick wheat noodles, usually in hot broth. More chewy and substantial than soba.
Wheat noodles in broth (shoyu / shio / miso / tonkotsu). Tsuzumi-soba in Takayama does pure chicken-shoyu — restrained, no chashu pile-on.
Tokyo's runny pan-fried batter dish — cabbage, meat or seafood, sauces, cooked at your table on a flat-top. The Tsukishima Monja Street is the home turf.
Savory pancake of cabbage, batter, and toppings. Osaka-style is mixed; Hiroshima-style is layered with noodles. Not on the trip route but you'll see it.
Vinegared rice topped or rolled with fish, vegetables, or egg. Nigiri = hand-shaped; chirashi = bowl of rice with fish scattered on top.
Sliced raw fish — no rice. A kaiseki course on its own.
Lightly battered, deep-fried seafood or vegetables. Dipped in tentsuyu sauce with grated daikon.
Hida-region specialty — beef, miso, leek, and shiitake grilled on a dried magnolia leaf over a tabletop brazier. The leaf perfumes the food.
Sweet-savory miso paste with mushrooms or vegetables, grilled on a hōba leaf. The classic Hida breakfast.
Buddhist temple vegetarian cuisine. No meat, fish, garlic, or onion. Highly refined despite the constraints. Shigetsu at Tenryu-ji is the Arashiyama anchor.
Kyoto-style home cooking — small plates of seasonal vegetables, often shown on a counter to pick from. Menami and Gion Kappa are the unfussy counter spots.
Traditional Japanese sweets — bean paste, mochi, jellies — designed to pair with green tea. Each season has its motifs. Toraya is the Tokyo flagship; Kanshundo, the Kyoto pick.
Fresh wagashi, made same-day, often artisan-shaped to match the season (a maple leaf in November). Higher tier than the boxed wagashi for gifting.
Skewered rice-flour dumplings, grilled. Mitarashi dango = with sweet soy glaze (Takayama's version is savory, not sweet).
Sweet-soy glaze on grilled dango. Sangawaya in Takayama makes the soy-only Takayama-style version (no sweetener).
Pounded glutinous rice, soft and chewy. Often filled with sweet bean paste (daifuku). Demachi Futaba's mame-mochi (bean mochi) is the Kyoto cult.
A jelly-like dessert from bracken-root starch, dusted with kinako (roasted soy flour) and kuromitsu (black sugar syrup). Cooler and silkier than rice mochi.
Osaka cousin of kushiage — skewered fried meat/veg. Not on the trip route.
Boiled tofu in a light dashi, served with condiments. A Kyoto specialty, especially in Arashiyama.
Tofu skin, the film that forms on heated soy milk. A Kyoto delicacy — order it fresh (nama-yuba), not dried.
Slow-simmered fish cakes, daikon, egg, tofu in dashi broth. Winter konbini classic; often sold at counters.
Japanese pub. Small plates, often alcohol-centric. This trip mostly skips them by design — but the food range is huge.
Old-school Japanese coffee house — siphon or pour-over coffee, kissaten-style egg sandwich, dark wood interiors. Kayaba Coffee in Yanaka, Inoda Coffee in Kyoto, Smart Coffee.
The basement food hall of a Japanese department store. International-airline-business-class level pre-made deli food, bento, wagashi. Isetan Shinjuku is the canonical pick.
Train-station bento. Each region has signature ones (Hida-gyu shigure in Takayama, miso-katsu sand in Nagoya). Eat on the train, not the platform.
Convenience store. 7-Eleven, FamilyMart, Lawson. Surprisingly excellent food — onigiri, sandos, oden, hot coffee, fresh fruit. Open 24/7.
Triangular rice ball with a filling (salmon = sake, pickled plum = umeboshi, tuna mayo) and a separate-sealed seaweed wrap. Opening sequence is printed on the wrapper.
Rolled sweet-savory omelet, sold by the slice or whole at markets. Tsukiji has Yamacho (caramelized-sweet) and Shouro (dashi-forward) right next to each other.
A set meal — main dish + rice + soup + pickles. The pragmatic Japanese lunch.
Thinly sliced beef + vegetables + tofu, simmered in a sweet-savory soy-sake-sugar broth at the table. Often dipped in raw beaten egg.
Hot-pot variant — diners swish thin meat slices through bubbling dashi at the table, then dip in ponzu or sesame sauce.
Sunken hearth in the floor of a traditional wooden house. Charcoal in the center, kettle suspended above. Hidatakayama Kyoya and Irori in Shirakawa-go both seat around one.
Powdered green tea, whisked into hot water until frothy. The bitter ceremonial form (usu-cha is thinner, koi-cha is thicker).
Roasted green tea — toasty, low caffeine, brown color. The non-alcoholic kaiseki pairing of choice.
Standard green tea — steamed whole leaves brewed in hot (not boiling) water. The daily tea.
Cold roasted-barley tea. Caffeine-free, summer staple, often served gratis at ryokans.
Green tea blended with roasted brown rice — toasty, mild, often served with rice meals.
Bullet train. Reserve seats via SmartEX or at JR ticket counters. The Tokaido Shinkansen is the Tokyo–Nagoya–Kyoto–Osaka spine.
Odakyu's premium limited-express train from Shinjuku to Hakone-Yumoto. Reserved seats only. The newer GSE has front-row observation seats.
Limited Express scenic train through the Hida River gorge — Nagoya → Takayama (Day 8) and Takayama → Kyoto direct (Day 11). Reserve seats on the right (D side) for the gorge view.
JR West's limited express from Kyoto to Kansai International Airport (KIX). ~75 min. The foreign-tourist one-way ticket is ¥2,200.
Japan Railways — six regional companies (JR East, Central, West, etc.). Operates Shinkansen, most major intercity lines, and the Yamanote loop in Tokyo.
Door-to-door luggage forwarding via Yamato Transport ("Kuroneko"). Drop bags at your hotel front desk; they arrive at the next hotel in 1–2 days. ~¥2,000/bag. Travel with carry-ons in between.
JR West's IC card (Kansai region). Works on every train, bus, vending machine, and konbini nationwide. Identical function to Suica/Pasmo.
JR East's IC card. Same as ICOCA / Pasmo — interchangeable nationwide. Add it to Apple Wallet on arrival.
Odakyu's mobile-ticket app for Romancecar bookings. ¥50 cheaper than counter-purchase.
JR Central's Shinkansen booking app — book Tokaido/Sanyo Shinkansen and limited expresses from your phone.
Train station. Suffix on place names (Tokyo-eki = Tokyo Station).
Street / avenue. Suffix on street names (Yasugawa-dōri = Yasugawa Street).
Small alley or lane. Hanami-koji in Gion, Pontocho-koji in central Kyoto.
A neighborhood block in the Japanese address system. Addresses go: city / ward / chōme / banchi (block number) / building number. Counterintuitive vs Western street-number addresses.
Block number within a chōme. Numbered by order of registration, not geography, so adjacent buildings are often non-adjacent numbers.
Foreign tourists can purchase tax-free over ¥5,000 per shop per day at participating stores. Present your passport; goods are bagged and shouldn't be opened until you leave Japan.
"Nightingale floor" — squeaky wooden corridor designed to alert occupants of intruders. Walk it slowly in Sanzen-in (Ohara) and listen — that chirp is the security system.
Japanese garden design principle where distant mountains or trees beyond the garden are visually incorporated into the composition. Tenryu-ji's Sogen-chi pond uses Arashiyama's hills this way.
National holiday on Nov 3. In 2026 it falls on a Tuesday, so domestic travelers extend it into a 4-day weekend — central Kyoto melts down. Day 12 of this trip escapes north to Ohara.
The takkyubin / luggage-forwarding company. "Kuroneko" = "black cat", the company mascot — black cat carrying a kitten.
Shinto deity or spirit — of mountains, rivers, rocks, ancestors. Shrines (jinja) house them. The central path through a torii belongs to the kami; walk to the side.
Evening illumination of temples / shrines / gardens during foliage or cherry-blossom seasons. Eikan-do, Sanzen-in, Hida-no-Sato all run them in November.
